perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be generally guided by principles inspired by the laws of the State of New York.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause clearly states New York law governs the contract. The modified text introduces ambiguity by using vague language like 'generally guided' and 'inspired by,' which weakens the governing law provision. This can lead to disputes over which laws apply and how they should be interpreted, making the contract difficult to enforce. Since the contract operates within the United States, this ambiguity can be challenged by existing legal precedents.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Choice of Law Principles and Contract Enforcement
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law allows parties to a contract to choose New York law to govern the agreement. The original clause clearly invokes this right. The modified clause, however, weakens this choice by suggesting the agreement is only 'generally guided' by New York law, creating uncertainty and potentially undermining the enforceability of the contract under New York law. This ambiguity contradicts the purpose of NY CLS Gen Oblig § 5-1401, which is to provide certainty in contractual relationships by allowing parties to specify the governing law.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 14 Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum In Certain Transactions up TITLE 14 Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum In Certain Transactions next SECTION 5-1402 Choice of forum This entry was published on 2018-08-31 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-1401 Choice of law General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 14 § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the original governing law provision with a vague reference to being 'generally guided by principles inspired by' New York law. This weakens the binding nature of New York law on the contract. NY CLS Gen Oblig § 5-1401 allows parties to a contract to choose New York law to govern their rights and duties. The modified clause undermines this choice by introducing uncertainty. The scraped snippet 1, specifically states 'the parties to any contract...may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part...'. The original text did this and was compliant, the changed text does not, thus creating a contradiction and making it more difficult to enforce under New York law.

perturbation.original_text: Sponsor will maintain accurate records with respect to the calculation of all payments due under this Agreement. Excite may, upon no less than thirty (30) days prior written notice to Sponsor, cause an independent Certified Public Accountant to inspect the records of Sponsor reasonably related to the calculation of such payments during Sponsor's normal business hours. The fees charged by such Certified Public Accountant in connection with the inspection will be paid by Excite unless the payments made to Excite are determined to have been less than ninety percent (90%) of the payment owed to Excite, in which case Sponsor will be responsible for the payment of the reasonable fees for such inspection.
perturbation.changed_text: Sponsor will endeavor to maintain records related to payments. Excite may request a review of Sponsor's records, subject to mutual agreement on the scope and timing. Costs associated with any such review will be discussed and allocated as deemed appropriate by both parties.
perturbation.explanation: The original text stipulates the maintenance of *accurate* records, provides a process for audits, and clearly defines who pays for the audit depending on its findings. The new text introduces vagueness by replacing 'maintain accurate records' with 'endeavor to maintain records,' implying a lower standard of accuracy. It replaces the right to an independent audit with a mere request, subject to 'mutual agreement,' giving Sponsor more control and potentially blocking an audit. Finally, it replaces the clear rule on audit costs with a vague statement about costs being 'discussed and allocated as deemed appropriate,' creating room for disagreement and potentially deterring Excite from conducting audits. This ambiguity weakens Excite's ability to verify payments, and enforce the payment terms in the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract and Duty of Good Faith
perturbation.law_explanation: The UCC § 1-304 states that every contract or duty within the UCC imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. By weakening the requirement to maintain accurate records and obscuring the audit process, the modified text allows for Sponsor to potentially avoid accurate payment calculations. This may hinder Excite's ability to enforce the payment terms and may constitute a breach of the duty of good faith. Although this contract may not be fully covered under the UCC as it involves services, the principle of good faith is generally applicable to contracts.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the principle of good faith as described in UCC § 1-304. The original text ensured accurate records and provided a clear audit process, which supports the enforcement of payment terms. The changed text weakens the requirement for accurate records to 'endeavor to maintain records,' and replaces the right to an independent audit with a request subject to mutual agreement. This change allows the Sponsor to potentially avoid accurate payment calculations and hinders Excite's ability to enforce payment terms, conflicting with the obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of the contract as stated in the snippet: 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.'

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees (i) that it will not disclose to any third party or use any Confidential Information disclosed to it by the other except as expressly permitted in this Agreement and (ii) that it will take all reasonable measures to maintain the confidentiality of all Confidential Information of the other party in its possession or control, which will in no event be less than the measures it uses to maintain the confidentiality of its own information of similar importance.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party will consider the other's information confidential and will endeavor not to disclose such information to third parties without due consideration.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a clear and strong obligation to protect confidential information using 'all reasonable measures,' and establishing a clear minimum standard for those measures. The modified text weakens this obligation significantly. By replacing 'agrees that it will not disclose' with 'will consider the other's information confidential and will endeavor not to disclose,' the obligation becomes aspirational rather than mandatory. The phrase 'due consideration' is vague and subjective, offering no clear standard for what constitutes appropriate protection of confidential information. This provides no verifiable metric for the security of the data, and makes it much easier to justify breaches of confidentiality, and this undermines the entire intent of the confidentiality clause.
perturbation.law_citation: New York State - Economic Espionage Act (Federal)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act protects trade secrets. While the modified clause doesn't explicitly authorize the theft of trade secrets, it weakens the protections to such a degree that it becomes easier to misappropriate or disclose them without clear legal repercussions. The weaker language and subjective standard of 'due consideration' makes it harder to prove that a party failed to take adequate measures to protect trade secrets, and it makes it harder to demonstrate that the defendant had the knowledge necessary to show intent to take a trade secret. This reduction in protection conflicts with the intent of the Economic Espionage Act, which is to provide strong legal safeguards for trade secrets.
perturbation.justification: The original contract text established a clear and strong obligation to protect confidential information, while the changed text weakens this obligation significantly, replacing mandatory terms with aspirational ones. The phrase 'due consideration' introduces a vague standard, making it harder to prove a failure to protect trade secrets, potentially conflicting with the intent of laws like the Economic Espionage Act, which aims to provide strong legal safeguards for trade secrets. The scraped snippet refers to the Economic Espionage Act of 1996 which indicates the importance of safeguarding trade secrets, and the changed text undermines this legal protection by weakening the confidentiality requirements, thus creating a legal contradiction under the Ambiguity Legal Contradiction definition.
